title: When does winter actually start and end?
text:
This project is inspired by FiveThirtyEight’s survey about when the four seasons begin: https://web.archive.org/web/20250722030131/https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/our-very-unscientific-poll-on-when-each-season-starts/
I thought that many of the markers they suggest to start the seasons don’t really work for Minnesota, especially for winter.
For example, they suggested “When the first snow falls” or “When the first snow sticks to the ground” as markers for the start of winter. Obviously, in Minnesota, the first true snowfall is frequently in late October. Anyone who remembers the Halloween Blizzard of 1991 will enthusiastically tell you about it. They also suggest March 1 as a start date for spring, and we all know that March 1 still feels like winter.
I decided to calculate the start and end of each season based on historical weather data for the Twin Cities area - that’s the metropolitan region of Minneapolis and St. Paul, Minnesota. Specifically, I want to look at the distribution of daily high temperatures: summer should be the quarter centered around the estimated hottest day of the year, and winter should be the quarter centered around the estiamted coldest day of the year.

title: When does winter actually start and end?
text:
This project is inspired by FiveThirtyEight’s survey about when the four seasons begin: https://web.archive.org/web/20250722030131/https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/our-very-unscientific-poll-on-when-each-season-starts/
I thought that many of the markers they suggest to start the seasons don’t really work for Minnesota, especially for winter.
For example, they suggested “When the first snow falls” or “When the first snow sticks to the ground” as markers for the start of winter. Obviously, in Minnesota, the first true snowfall is frequently in late October. Anyone who remembers the Halloween Blizzard of 1991 will enthusiastically tell you about it. They also suggest March 1 as a start date for spring, and we all know that March 1 still feels like winter.
I decided to calculate the start and end of each season based on historical weather data for the Twin Cities area - that’s the metropolitan region of Minneapolis and St. Paul, Minnesota. Specifically, I want to look at the distribution of daily high temperatures: summer should be the quarter centered around the estimated hottest day of the year, and winter should be the quarter centered around the estiamted coldest day of the year.

title: When does winter actually start and end?
text:
Import and clean data
Historical weather data for the Twin Cities comes from the Minnesota Department of Natural Resources. It is publicly available here: https://www.dnr.state.mn.us/climate/twin_cities/listings.html
I downloaded the data from the very beginning (October 1, 1872) through yesterday (March 16, 2026). Let’s import and examine the data:

# Use the here package to manage filepaths
here::i_am("seasons.qmd")

here() starts at D:/quarto_website2

weather_raw <- readr::read_csv(
  here::here("data/Twin Cities Historical Climate Data.csv"),
  #' Import the first two columns as date and numeric format, and no other
  #' columns
  col_types = readr::cols_only(readr::col_date(), readr::col_number()),
  # Rename the columns
  skip = 1,
  col_names = c("date", "high_temp"),
  # Per the data documentation, interpret "M" as missing (NA)
  na = c("", "M"),
  skip_empty_rows = TRUE
)

gtsummary::tbl_summary(
  weather_raw,
  type = ~"continuous2",
  statistic = ~ c("{min}", "{max}")
)











Characteristic
N = 56,049




date




    Min
1872-10-01


    Max
2026-03-16


high_temp




    Min
-20


    Max
108


    Unknown
1







For a first cleaning step, let’s:

drop the records that are missing temperature data,
make sure the data is sorted by date,
add a column to convert every date to the year 2024, so we can see it collapsed by month & day across all years, and then
check the distribution


weather <- weather_raw |>
  tidyr::drop_na() |>
  dplyr::arrange(date) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    date24 = lubridate::make_date(
      year = 2024,
      month = lubridate::month(date),
      day = lubridate::day(date)
    )
  )

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data = weather) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = date24, y = high_temp), alpha = 0.07) +
  scale_x_date(
    name = "Day of the year (1871-2025)",
    date_labels = "%b",
    date_breaks = "1 month"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Daily high temperature (F)")








We can see the difference between seasons very clearly! We can also see a very thin line on February 29 where there is less data.

title: When does winter actually start and end?
text:
Astronomy > calendar
However, that means we have a problem: taking simply the day and month of every reading doesn’t account for leap years. The data on a leap year is shifted slightly compared to a non-leap-year.
We know that the temperature isn’t actually a function of the date, but a function of the Earth’s position relative to the Sun. To approximate that, we could standardize each date to the number of days after the winter solstice each year. We can scrape historical solstice dates from the US Navy’s website using the rvest package:

extract_solstice_data <- function(year_chr) {
  # Scrape data from the HTML table on the Navy webpage for each year
  scraped_data <- glue::glue(
    "https://aa.usno.navy.mil/calculated/seasons?year={year_chr}&tz=6.00&tz_sign=-1&tz_label=true&dst=true&submit=Get+Data"
  ) |>
    rvest::read_html() |>
    rvest::html_node("table") |>
    rvest::html_table()

  # Rename columns
  colnames(scraped_data) <- c("phenomenon", "date", "time")

  # Filter to only include the winter solstice date
  scraped_data |>
    dplyr::filter(phenomenon == "Solstice") |>
    dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(date, "December"))
}

# Run this function on every year from 1871 through 2026
years <- c(1871:2026)

#' This takes a lot of processing time to run, and it's rude to repeatedly
#' scrape the same website, so I'm not actually going to run it every time I
#' render this page - I ran it once and saved the dataset.

if (FALSE) {
  solstice_data <- purrr::map(years, extract_solstice_data, .progress = TRUE)

  # Save
  saveRDS(solstice_data, here::here("data/solstice_data.RData"))
} else {
  solstice_data <- readRDS(here::here("data/solstice_data.RData"))
}

solstice_data2 <- dplyr::bind_rows(solstice_data) |>
  # Keep only the date column
  dplyr::select(date) |>
  # Convert character to date and set year
  dplyr::mutate(
    solstice_date = lubridate::ymd(date),
    year = lubridate::year(solstice_date),
    .keep = "unused"
  )

print(solstice_data2)

# A tibble: 156 × 2
   solstice_date  year
   <date>        <dbl>
 1 1871-12-21     1871
 2 1872-12-21     1872
 3 1873-12-21     1873
 4 1874-12-21     1874
 5 1875-12-21     1875
 6 1876-12-21     1876
 7 1877-12-21     1877
 8 1878-12-21     1878
 9 1879-12-21     1879
10 1880-12-21     1880
# ℹ 146 more rows


Although the top slice of this dataset makes it look like the winter solstice is always on December 21, that is not true! It is occasionally on Dec 22 and very rarely on Dec 20 or Dec 23. I also set the Navy website to account for Daylight Savings Time (as much as possible) and our time zone (Central time, UTC-6).
Now we can merge the solstice dates into the dataset, and convert the dates into days after the winter solstice:

# Set random seed based on current time (as I'm writing) in milliseconds
# format(Sys.time(), digits = 10)
# "2026-03-12 09:54:33.658463"
set.seed(658463)

weather2 <- weather |>
  #' Merge the solstice data in twice - for each record, we want both the
  #' current year and the previous year's solstice dates, so we can choose the
  #' closer one
  dplyr::mutate(
    year = lubridate::year(date),
    last_year = year - 1
  ) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    solstice_data2,
    by = dplyr::join_by(year)
  ) |>
  dplyr::left_join(
    solstice_data2 |>
      dplyr::rename(last_solstice_date = solstice_date),
    by = dplyr::join_by(last_year == year)
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    solstice_days_this_year = lubridate::interval(solstice_date, date) /
      lubridate::days(),
    solstice_days_last_year = lubridate::interval(
      last_solstice_date,
      date
    ) /
      lubridate::days(),
    random_number = runif(n = nrow(weather)),
    solstice_days = dplyr::case_when(
      #' Choose whichever solstice date is closest (before or after) the record 
      #' date
      abs(solstice_days_this_year) <
        abs(solstice_days_last_year) ~ solstice_days_this_year,
      abs(solstice_days_last_year) <
        abs(solstice_days_this_year) ~ solstice_days_last_year,
      #' If they are equidistant (approximately June 21), choose randomly
      #' between 183 and -183
      random_number < 0.5 ~ solstice_days_this_year,
      .default = solstice_days_last_year
    )
  )

weather2 |>
  dplyr::select(
    date,
    solstice_date,
    last_solstice_date,
    solstice_days
  ) |>
  print()

# A tibble: 56,048 × 4
   date       solstice_date last_solstice_date solstice_days
   <date>     <date>        <date>                     <dbl>
 1 1872-10-01 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -81
 2 1872-10-02 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -80
 3 1872-10-03 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -79
 4 1872-10-04 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -78
 5 1872-10-05 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -77
 6 1872-10-06 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -76
 7 1872-10-07 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -75
 8 1872-10-08 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -74
 9 1872-10-09 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -73
10 1872-10-10 1872-12-21    1871-12-21                   -72
# ℹ 56,038 more rows


Now our distribution looks roughly the same, just flipped because the center of the x-axis is now the winter solstice:

ggplot(data = weather2) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, y = high_temp), alpha = 0.07) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Daily high temperature (F)") +
  labs(x = "Days after the winter solstice")

title: When does winter actually start and end?
text:
Predicted temperatures
Now, we want to summarize the temperature distribution for each day. We could simply take the mean of each day, but the resulting curve is still jagged:

weather_avg <- weather2 |>
  dplyr::summarize(
    avg_high_temp = mean(high_temp),
    .by = solstice_days
  )

ggplot(data = weather_avg) +
  geom_line(mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, y = avg_high_temp)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Average daily high temperature (F)") +
  labs(x = "Days after the winter solstice")








Another method would be to add a geom_smooth() line to display a predicted temperature line:

ggplot(data = weather2) +
  geom_point(
    mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, y = high_temp),
    alpha = 0.07
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "Daily high temperature (F)") +
  geom_smooth(mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, y = high_temp)) +
  labs(x = "Days after the winter solstice")

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'gam' and formula = 'y ~ s(x, bs = "cs")'









This is much clearer, but we can’t extract the predicted temperature values from the graph. Instead of using geom_smooth(), we can calculate the values of that predicted temperature line using the same method. Per the geom_smooth() documentation:

… [T]he smoothing method is chosen based on the size of the largest group (across all panels). stats::loess() is used for less than 1,000 observations; otherwise mgcv::gam() is used with formula = y ~ s(x, bs = "cs") with method = "REML".

In other words, because our dataset has well over 1,000 observations, geom_smooth() uses a generalized additive model. The tidymodels package parsnip includes a generalized additive model function parsnip::gen_additive_mod() that defaults to the mgcv engine. Let’s build and fit a model using the parsnip workflow:

# Create the model specification
gam_spec <- parsnip::gen_additive_mod() |>
  # method parameter ("REML") specified in the geom_smooth() documentation:
  parsnip::set_engine("mgcv", method = "REML") |>
  parsnip::set_mode("regression")
gam_spec

GAM Model Specification (regression)

Engine-Specific Arguments:
  method = REML

Computational engine: mgcv 

# Fit the model to our dataset
gam_fit <- gam_spec |>
  parsnip::fit(
    # The formula specified in the geom_smooth() documentation:
    # y ~ s(x, bs = "cs")
    high_temp ~ s(solstice_days, bs = "cs"),
    data = weather2
  )
gam_fit

parsnip model object


Family: gaussian 
Link function: identity 

Formula:
high_temp ~ s(solstice_days, bs = "cs")

Estimated degrees of freedom:
8.97  total = 9.97 

REML score: 211965.6     

# Get predicted high temperatures
predicted_temps <- predict(gam_fit, new_data = weather2)

weather3 <- weather2 |>
  dplyr::bind_cols(predicted_temps) |>
  dplyr::rename(predicted_high_temp = .pred)

weather_pred <- weather3 |>
  dplyr::distinct(solstice_days, predicted_high_temp)
weather_pred

# A tibble: 367 × 2
   solstice_days predicted_high_temp
           <dbl>               <dbl>
 1           -81                65.7
 2           -80                65.2
 3           -79                64.8
 4           -78                64.3
 5           -77                63.9
 6           -76                63.4
 7           -75                63.0
 8           -74                62.5
 9           -73                62.0
10           -72                61.6
# ℹ 357 more rows


Let’s see how our model’s predicted high temperatures (in red) compares to the original geom_smooth() line (in blue):

ggplot() +
  labs(
    x = "Days after the winter solstice",
    y = "Predicted daily high temperature (F)"
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = weather3,
    mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, y = high_temp),
    alpha = 0.07
  ) +
  geom_smooth(
    data = weather3,
    mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, y = high_temp)
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = weather_pred,
    mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, y = predicted_high_temp),
    color = "red"
  )

`geom_smooth()` using method = 'gam' and formula = 'y ~ s(x, bs = "cs")'









Looks really close to me! We can also check our predicted temperatures against the actual temperatures - we can see the top and bottom of the curve where the predicted temperatures “flatten” at just above 20 and 80 degrees:

ggplot(weather3, aes(x = high_temp, y = predicted_high_temp)) +
  # Create a diagonal line:
  geom_abline(lty = 2) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.03) +
  labs(y = "Predicted High Temperature (F)", x = "Actual Temperature (F)")








But there is still variation in the predicted temperatures if we look closely:

weather3 |>
  dplyr::filter(predicted_high_temp < 25) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = high_temp, y = predicted_high_temp)) +
  # Create a diagonal line:
  geom_abline(lty = 2) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.1) +
  labs(y = "Predicted High Temperature (F)", x = "Actual Temperature (F)")

title: When does winter actually start and end?
text:
Season dates
Now, we can identify the estimated coldest and hottest quarters of the year.

weather_pred2 <- weather_pred |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    season = dplyr::case_when(
      predicted_high_temp < quantile(predicted_high_temp, 0.25) ~ "Winter",
      predicted_high_temp > quantile(predicted_high_temp, 0.75) ~ "Summer",
      solstice_days > 0 ~ "Spring",
      solstice_days < 0 ~ "Fall"
    ) |>
      forcats::fct(levels = c("Winter", "Spring", "Summer", "Fall"))
  )

gtsummary::tbl_summary(
  weather_pred2,
  include = solstice_days,
  type = ~"continuous2",
  statistic = ~ c("{min}", "{max}"),
  label = ~"Days after the winter solstice",
  by = season
) |>
  gtsummary::modify_footnote_header(
    footnote = "The min and max days for summer look strange because early summer is positive (167 to 183 days after the winter solstice), and then becomes negative around the summer solstice (183 to 106 days *before* the winter solstice).",
    columns = "stat_3"
  )














Characteristic
Winter
N = 92
Spring
N = 96
Summer
N = 921
Fall
N = 87




Days after the winter solstice










    Min
-20
72
-183
-107


    Max
71
167
183
-21



1 The min and max days for summer look strange because early summer is positive (167 to 183 days after the winter solstice), and then becomes negative around the summer solstice (183 to 106 days before the winter solstice).







ggplot(
  mapping = aes(
    x = solstice_days,
    y = predicted_high_temp,
    color = season,
  )
) +
  #' Draw as two layers so that ggplot doesn't try to connect both summer lines
  #' across the axis
  geom_line(
    data = weather_pred2 |>
      dplyr::filter(solstice_days <= 166),
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = weather_pred2 |>
      dplyr::filter(solstice_days > 166),
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Days after the winter solstice",
    y = "Predicted high temperature (F)",
    color = "Season"
  )








It surprised me somewhat that spring is longer than fall - I would have guessed that the coldest and hottest quarters of the year would be exactly opposite each other. But experientially, it sounds correct! Fall always feels like a faster transition than spring. On a radial chart, it’s easier to see how this works:

ggplot() +
  geom_rect(
    data = weather_pred2,
    mapping = aes(x = solstice_days, fill = season),
    y = 1,
    width = 2,
    ymin = 0,
    ymax = 1
  ) +
  #' These breaks are exact quarters (91.5 days apart) starting with 20.5 days
  #' after the winter solstice, which is the division between fall and winter
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(-112, -20.5, 71, 162.5)) +
  labs(x = "Days after the winter solstice", fill = "Season", y = NULL) +
  # Rotate the graph so that the break at 20.5 days points downward.
  coord_radial(expand = FALSE, start = (20.5 / 366) * 2 * pi) +
  theme_sub_axis_y(text = element_blank(), ticks = element_blank()) +
  theme_sub_legend(position = "bottom")








Then, we convert “days after the winter solstice” to typical dates:

# Take the most common date for each number of days after the winter solstice
solstice_to_date <- weather3 |>
  dplyr::count(solstice_days, date24) |>
  dplyr::filter(
    n == max(n),
    .by = solstice_days
  ) |>
  # Reduce to 1:1 solstice days to dates, for 365 rows exactly
  dplyr::filter(
    n == max(n),
    .by = date24
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(!n)


weather_pred3 <- weather_pred2 |>
  dplyr::right_join(solstice_to_date, by = dplyr::join_by(solstice_days)) |>
  # Adjust the year on early winter dates to 2023 so they appear in order
  dplyr::mutate(
    date_adj = dplyr::if_else(
      season == "Winter" & lubridate::month(date24) > 6,
      date24 - lubridate::years(1),
      date24
    )
  )

gtsummary::tbl_summary(
  weather_pred3,
  include = c(date_adj, predicted_high_temp),
  type = ~"continuous2",
  statistic = ~ c("{min}", "{median}", "{max}"),
  label = list(
    date_adj = "Month and day",
    predicted_high_temp = "Predicted high temperature (F)"
  ),
  digits = list(
    date_adj = scales::label_date(format = "%b %d"),
    predicted_high_temp = scales::label_number(accuracy = 0.1)
  ),
  by = season
)














Characteristic
Winter
N = 92
Spring
N = 96
Summer
N = 90
Fall
N = 87




Month and day










    Min
Dec 01
Mar 03
Jun 07
Sep 05


    Median
Jan 15
Apr 19
Jul 21
Oct 18


    Max
Mar 02
Jun 06
Sep 04
Nov 30


Predicted high temperature (F)










    Min
21.8
32.5
75.7
32.5


    Median
24.3
57.8
81.7
57.5


    Max
32.1
75.7
83.6
75.4






ggplot() +
  geom_line(
    data = weather_pred3,
    mapping = aes(
      x = date_adj,
      y = predicted_high_temp,
      color = season,
    )
  ) +
  scale_x_date(date_labels = "%b") +
  labs(
    x = "Days after the winter solstice",
    y = "Predicted high temperature (F)",
    color = "Season"
  ) +
  theme_sub_legend(position = "bottom")








I have to admit, other than the little bump in the prediction curve around the summer solstice, this is really satisfying to me.
The final results are:

Winter: December 1 through March 2
Spring: March 3 through June 6
Summer: June 7 through September 4
Fall: September 5 through November 30

Thanks for reading!

title: Lily Dunk
text: Hi, I’m Lily, a data analyst in Minneapolis, MN. Contact me at lily.b.dunk@protonmail.com.

title: Lily Dunk
text:
Work Experience

Minneapolis, MN | A research, evaluation, and consulting firm specializing in public health program evaluations.

Lien, B., Dunk, L., & Richter, S. (2022). Let SAS do the work for you: Tips and tricks for turning SAS output into client-ready tables. Western Users of SAS Software 2022 Conference Proceedings. https://www.wuss.org/proceedings/2022/WUSS-2022-Paper-112.pdf.

title: Does Gen Z drink less than other generations did?
text:
In the past few years, I’ve read various news articles about a supposed trend: that Gen Z (people born between 1997 and 2012) drink less alcohol than previous generations did at the same age:

The Conversation, “The rise of ‘sober curiosity:’ Why Gen Zers are reducing their alcohol consumption” (November 2024)
Time Magazine, “Why Gen Z Is Drinking Less” (January 2025)
Gallup, “U.S. Drinking Rate at New Low as Alcohol Concerns Surge” (August 2025)
Wall Street Journal, “Why Generation Z Doesn’t Drink Much” (September 2025)
USA Today, “Gen Z is ghosting alcohol and changing what a ‘night out’ looks like” (March 2026)

These articles reference data from various surveys, and I was curious if I could identify that same trend in the CDC’s annual Behavioral Health Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) survey.
The main questions these articles raise for me are:
Compared to previous generations at the same age…

Is Gen Z more likely to be completely sober? (meaning, to not drink at all)?
Does Gen Z drink less often?
Is Gen Z less likely to binge drink?

and, if so, is there a greater difference in Gen Z than in other generations? In other words, can the difference be explained by all Americans reducing their drinking, or is this a Gen Z-specific phenomenon?

title: Does Gen Z drink less than other generations did?
text:
In the past few years, I’ve read various news articles about a supposed trend: that Gen Z (people born between 1997 and 2012) drink less alcohol than previous generations did at the same age:

The Conversation, “The rise of ‘sober curiosity:’ Why Gen Zers are reducing their alcohol consumption” (November 2024)
Time Magazine, “Why Gen Z Is Drinking Less” (January 2025)
Gallup, “U.S. Drinking Rate at New Low as Alcohol Concerns Surge” (August 2025)
Wall Street Journal, “Why Generation Z Doesn’t Drink Much” (September 2025)
USA Today, “Gen Z is ghosting alcohol and changing what a ‘night out’ looks like” (March 2026)

These articles reference data from various surveys, and I was curious if I could identify that same trend in the CDC’s annual Behavioral Health Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) survey.
The main questions these articles raise for me are:
Compared to previous generations at the same age…

Is Gen Z more likely to be completely sober? (meaning, to not drink at all)?
Does Gen Z drink less often?
Is Gen Z less likely to binge drink?

and, if so, is there a greater difference in Gen Z than in other generations? In other words, can the difference be explained by all Americans reducing their drinking, or is this a Gen Z-specific phenomenon?

title: Does Gen Z drink less than other generations did?
text:
About the data
Data from the annual BRFSS surveys are publicly available from the CDC. The most recent dataset is from the 2024 survey. Due to changes in survey methodology, the CDC advises that recent BRFSS data cannot be directly compared with BRFSS data older than the 2011 survey. So we will compare the 2024 and 2011 surveys.
The Pew Research Center uses a standard definition of each generation by birth year:



Generation
Birth years
Age in 2024




Generation Z
1997 onward
21 to 27


Millennials
1981 to 1996
27 to 43


Generation X
1965 to 1980
43 to 59


Baby Boomers
1946 to 1964
59 to 78


Silent Generation
1928 to 1945
78 to 96



Gen Z was born from 1997 onward, so in 2024, the oldest members of Gen Z would have been age 27. Millennials born in 1996 could also have been age 27 in 2024 depending on their birthdate, but for the sake of simplicity, I will group 27-year-old participants with Gen Z.
I also want to exclude people under the legal drinking age, because I suspect that underage drinking could change because of changes to law enforcement, university discipline policies, etc. So our Gen Z sample will be ages 21 to 27, and everyone else will be ages 27+.
This means that our two samples of people ages 21 to 27 will include:

those born between 1997 and 2003 (Gen Z), surveyed in 2024, compared to
those born between 1984 and 1990 (Millennials), surveyed in 2011

The BRFSS variables we can use are:









2011 variable name
2024 variable name
Label
Note




_IMPAGE
_AGE80
2011: Age value used to determine age groups; 2024: Imputed Age value collapsed above 80
The 2011 dataset does not collapse high ages, but this shouldn’t affect our analysis because we are only interested in young people. Both samples are imputed, meaning that if a participant did not report their age, the CDC substituted a statistical estimate of their age.


DROCDY3_
DROCDY4_
Computed drink-occasions-per-day
Calculated from the number of days participants reported drinking in the past 30 days. This is essentially the proportion of days that the participant drinks.


_RFBING5
_RFBING6
Binge Drinking Calculated Variable: “Binge drinkers (males having five or more drinks on one occasion, females having four or more drinks on one occasion)”
i.e., reported binge drinking at least once within the past 30 days. Calculated variable based on ALCDAY4/ALCDAY5 and a question asking the number of binge drinking occasions in the last 30 days.

title: Does Gen Z drink less than other generations did?
Does Gen Z drink less than other generations did?
text:
Data cleaning
First, we’ll combine our datasets. We’ll standardize the variable names for the variables we’ll need, and add a variable to indicate the sample year.

# 2024
brfss24 <- brfss24_raw |>
  dplyr::rename(
    age = `_AGE80`,
    drink_days = `DROCDY4_`,
    binge_drink = `_RFBING6`
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(age, drink_days, binge_drink, `_STSTR`, `_LLCPWT`) |>
  dplyr::mutate(sample_year = "2024")

# 2011
brfss11 <- brfss11_raw |>
  dplyr::rename(
    age = `_IMPAGE`,
    drink_days = `DROCDY3_`,
    binge_drink = `_RFBING5`
  ) |>
  dplyr::select(age, drink_days, binge_drink, `_STSTR`, `_LLCPWT`) |>
  dplyr::mutate(sample_year = "2011")

# Combine
brfss1 <- list(brfss24, brfss11) |>
  dplyr::bind_rows() |>
  dplyr::rename(
    strata = `_STSTR`,
    weight = `_LLCPWT`
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    sample_year = forcats::fct(sample_year, levels = c("2011", "2024"))
  )

brfss1 |>
  dplyr::count(sample_year)

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  sample_year      n
  <fct>        <int>
1 2011        506467
2 2024        457670


Then, we’ll use the CDC’s documentation on BRFSS complex sampling weight methods to adjust the weights now that the sample is combined:

# Get the proportion of each sample year in the overall sample
prop11 <- (brfss1 |>
  dplyr::filter(sample_year == "2011") |>
  nrow()) /
  nrow(brfss1)
prop11

[1] 0.5253061

prop24 <- (brfss1 |>
  dplyr::filter(sample_year == "2024") |>
  nrow()) /
  nrow(brfss1)
prop24

[1] 0.4746939

prop11 + prop24

[1] 1

# Multiply the weights of each year by that proportion
brfss2 <- brfss1 |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    weight_adj = dplyr::case_when(
      sample_year == "2011" ~ weight * prop11,
      sample_year == "2024" ~ weight * prop24
    )
  )

#' Adjust the weights so that the total weighted frequency matches the actual
#' frequency
brfss3 <- brfss2 |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    final_weight = weight_adj * (nrow(brfss2) / sum(brfss2$weight_adj))
  )

nrow(brfss3)

[1] 964137

sum(brfss3$final_weight)

[1] 964137


Next, we’ll recode the age and alcohol variables based on the CDC’s codebooks for each sample. For the proportion of days that participants drink, we’ll convert to the number of days that participants drink per month (30 days) for clarity.

brfss <- brfss3 |>
  dplyr::filter(age >= 21) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    age_group = dplyr::case_when(
      age <= 27 ~ "Age 21-27",
      age >= 28 ~ "Age 28+"
    ) |>
      forcats::fct(levels = c("Age 21-27", "Age 28+")),
    #' Per the CDC's data documentation, 900 indicates "Don't know/Not sure" or
    #' "Refused/Missing", so we will recode it to NA
    drink_days = dplyr::if_else(
      drink_days == 900,
      NA,
      drink_days
    ) * (30/100), # divide by 100 to get proportion, multiply by 30 days
    drink = drink_days > 0,
    binge_drink = dplyr::recode_values(
      binge_drink,
      1 ~ FALSE,
      2 ~ TRUE,
      9 ~ NA
    )
  )

# Unweighted:
gtsummary::tbl_summary(
  brfss,
  include = c(age_group, drink_days, drink, binge_drink),
  statistic = list(drink_days = "{mean}"),
  digits = list(drink_days = 1)
)











Characteristic
N = 943,1351




age_group




    Age 21-27
58,410 (6.2%)


    Age 28+
884,725 (94%)


drink_days
4.9


    Unknown
75,528


drink
446,621 (51%)


    Unknown
75,528


binge_drink
112,593 (13%)


    Unknown
82,096



1 n (%); Mean








Finally, we’ll create a survey design object using the survey package, so that all of our analyses will integrate BRFSS strata and weights:

options(survey.lonely.psu = "adjust")

brfss_svy <- survey::svydesign(
  ids = ~1,
  strata = ~strata,
  weights = ~final_weight,
  data = brfss,
  check.strata = FALSE,
  nest = FALSE
)

# Weighted:
gtsummary::tbl_svysummary(
  brfss_svy,
  include = c(age_group, drink_days, drink, binge_drink),
  statistic = list(drink_days = "{mean}"),
  digits = ~ 1
)











Characteristic
N = 910,1771




age_group




    Age 21-27
112,778.4 (12.4%)


    Age 28+
797,398.3 (87.6%)


drink_days
4.7


    Unknown
85,665


drink
447,594.1 (54.3%)


    Unknown
85,665


binge_drink
137,939.0 (16.9%)


    Unknown
94,032



1 n (%); Mean

title: Does Gen Z drink less than other generations did?
text:
Analysis
Now we’re ready to test our hypotheses.
First, we’ll check if our hypothesis is plausible. Without any statistical testing, let’s compare the difference in alcohol use by age group and year:

gtsummary::tbl_strata(
  brfss_svy,
  strata = age_group,
  .tbl_fun = function(x) {
    gtsummary::tbl_svysummary(
      x,
      include = c(drink, drink_days, binge_drink),
      by = sample_year,
      statistic = list(drink_days = "{mean}"),
      digits = ~ 1
    )
  }
)

Here’s a simplified version of the same table, adding the differences between age groups and sample years:


Code
summary_df1 <- brfss_svy |>
  srvyr::as_survey_design() |>
  srvyr::summarize(
    drink_p = srvyr::survey_mean(drink, na.rm = TRUE),
    drink_days_mean = srvyr::survey_mean(drink_days, na.rm = TRUE),
    binge_drink_p = srvyr::survey_mean(binge_drink, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = c(age_group, sample_year)
  ) |>
  dplyr::ungroup() |>
  dplyr::select(!dplyr::ends_with("_se")) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(drink_p, drink_days_mean, binge_drink_p),
    names_to = "varname"
  )

# Difference between age groups
summary_df2 <- summary_df1 |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = age_group, values_from = value) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    agedif = `Age 28+` - `Age 21-27`
  ) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = !c(sample_year, varname),
    names_to = "age_group"
  ) |>
  tidyr::pivot_wider(
    names_from = sample_year,
    values_from = value,
    names_prefix = "p"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    yrdif = p2024 - p2011
  )

summary_table <- summary_df2 |>
  gt::gt(row_group_as_column = TRUE) |>
  gt::tab_row_group(
    label = "% who drink",
    rows = varname == "drink_p",
    id = "drink_p"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_row_group(
    label = "Mean drinking days per month",
    rows = varname == "drink_days_mean",
    id = "drink_days_mean"
  ) |>
  gt::tab_row_group(
    label = "% who binge drink",
    rows = varname == "binge_drink_p",
    id = "binge_drink_p"
  ) |>
  gt::row_group_order(c("drink_p", "drink_days_mean", "binge_drink_p")) |>
  # Column headers
  gt::cols_hide("varname") |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    location = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = c(1:4))
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(style = "italic"),
    location = gt::cells_column_labels(columns = 5)
  ) |>
  gt::cols_label(
    age_group = "Age group",
    p2011 = "2011",
    p2024 = "2024",
    yrdif = "Difference between sample years"
  ) |>
  # Percent formatting
  gt::fmt(
    columns = c(p2011, p2024),
    rows = age_group != "agedif" & varname != "drink_days_mean",
    fns = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 0.1)
  ) |>
  gt::fmt(
    columns = c(p2011, p2024),
    rows = age_group != "agedif" & varname == "drink_days_mean",
    fns = scales::label_number(accuracy = 0.1)
  ) |>
  gt::fmt(
    columns = c(p2011, p2024),
    rows = age_group == "agedif" & varname != "drink_days_mean",
    fns = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 0.1, style_positive = "plus")
  ) |>
  gt::fmt(
    columns = c(p2011, p2024),
    rows = age_group == "agedif" & varname == "drink_days_mean",
    fns = scales::label_number(accuracy = 0.1, style_positive = "plus")
  ) |>
  gt::fmt(
    columns = yrdif,
    rows = varname != "drink_days_mean",
    fns = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 0.1, style_positive = "plus")
  ) |>
  gt::fmt(
    columns = yrdif,
    rows = varname == "drink_days_mean",
    fns = scales::label_number(accuracy = 0.1, style_positive = "plus")
  ) |>
  # Cell styles
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(style = "italic"),
    location = gt::cells_body(columns = yrdif)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(style = "italic"),
    location = gt::cells_body(rows = age_group == "agedif")
  ) |>
  gt::text_replace("agedif", "Difference between age groups")

summary_table

We can see that both age groups reduced their drinking across all three variables, and the Age 21-27 group reduced their drinking more. So our hypothesis is plausible - we’ll just need to determine if the difference-in-difference is statistically significant.

Regression model walkthrough
For each variable, we’re not just testing if drinking increased from 2011 to 2024; we’re testing if drinking increased from 2011 to 2024 more for the 21-27 age group than it did for the 28+ age group. Since we are looking at the relationship between three different variables (alcohol use, age group, and sample year), we’ll use a regression analysis instead of a more typical statistical test like a chi-squared test or a t-test.
We’ll create a generalized linear model using the survey package to predict each variable based on age group, sample year, and an interaction between the two. We can then use the regression coefficients to understand how those predictors affect the outcome.

brfss_glm1 <- survey::svyglm(
  formula = drink ~ age_group * sample_year,
  design = brfss_svy
)
brfss_glm1

Stratified Independent Sampling design (with replacement)
survey::svydesign(ids = ~1, strata = ~strata, weights = ~final_weight, 
    data = brfss, check.strata = FALSE, nest = FALSE)

Call:  svyglm(formula = drink ~ age_group * sample_year, design = brfss_svy)

Coefficients:
                     (Intercept)                  age_groupAge 28+  
                         0.69406                          -0.15205  
                 sample_year2024  age_groupAge 28+:sample_year2024  
                        -0.09779                           0.06977  

Degrees of Freedom: 867606 Total (i.e. Null);  864080 Residual
  (75528 observations deleted due to missingness)
Null Deviance:      212000 
Residual Deviance: 210300   AIC: NA




Code
# Colors
teal <- "#66C2A5"
darkteal <- "#1B9E77"
orange <- "#FC8D62"
darkorange <- "#D95F02"
purple <- "#8DA0CB"
darkpurple <- "#7570B3"
pink <- "#E78AC3"
darkpink <- "#E7298A"

# Output table
brfss_glm1 |>
  broom::tidy() |>
  gt::gt() |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    location = gt::cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(estimate, statistic),
    decimals = 3,
    suffixing = TRUE
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = std.error, n_sigfig = 3, suffixing = TRUE) |>
  gt::fmt(columns = p.value, fns = scales::label_pvalue()) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkteal, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 2, rows = 1)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkorange, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 2, rows = 2)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkpurple, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 2, rows = 3)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkpink, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 2, rows = 4)
  )








term

p.value




(Intercept)
0.694

−0.098

<0.001


age_groupAge 28+:sample_year2024
0.070
0.00789
8.843
<0.001







The intercept is the estimate for the baseline groups: age 21-27 and sample year 2011.
The age_group coefficient is the estimated difference between the age groups when the sample year is kept constant (2011). The sample_year coefficient is the estimated difference between the sample years when the age group is kept constant (Age 21-27). The p-values for both of these coefficients are <0.001, so we know that there is a significant difference between the age groups in 2011 and a significant difference between the sample years for Age 21-27.
The interaction coefficient tells us about both variables. This is the difference between the differences: Between 2011 and 2024, the proportion of participants who drink decreased by 7% less for Age 28+ than it did for Age 21-27. And because the p-value for the interaction coefficient is also <0.001, we know that the difference is statistically significant.
Here is the same simplified table as above, but with the text colors changed to match the regression coefficients:


Code

  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkteal, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 3, rows = 1)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkorange, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 3, rows = 3)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkpurple, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 5, rows = 1)
  ) |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(color = darkpink, weight = "bold"),
    locations = gt::cells_body(columns = 5, rows = 3)
  )

Our first research question was: Is Gen Z more likely to be completely sober? (meaning, to not drink at all)? We’re using “did not drink in the past 30 days” as a proxy here, but the answer is yes.
Let’s repeat the same process for our other two variables.


Drinking days
Mean days per month that participants drink:

brfss_glm2 <- survey::svyglm(
  formula = drink_days ~ age_group * sample_year,
  design = brfss_svy
)
brfss_glm2

Stratified Independent Sampling design (with replacement)
survey::svydesign(ids = ~1, strata = ~strata, weights = ~final_weight, 
    data = brfss, check.strata = FALSE, nest = FALSE)

Call:  svyglm(formula = drink_days ~ age_group * sample_year, design = brfss_svy)

Coefficients:
                     (Intercept)                  age_groupAge 28+  
                          5.3005                           -0.2958  
                 sample_year2024  age_groupAge 28+:sample_year2024  
                         -1.5118                            0.9066  

Degrees of Freedom: 867606 Total (i.e. Null);  864080 Residual
  (75528 observations deleted due to missingness)
Null Deviance:      50860000 
Residual Deviance: 50730000     AIC: NA

brfss_glm2 |>
  broom::tidy() |>
  gt::gt() |>
  gt::tab_style(
    style = gt::cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    location = gt::cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(
    columns = c(estimate, statistic),
    decimals = 3,
    suffixing = TRUE
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = std.error, n_sigfig = 3, suffixing = TRUE) |>
  gt::fmt(columns = p.value, fns = scales::label_pvalue())

While the difference of differences here is statistically significant, it’s less than 1 day. Let’s look at the distributions of drinking days per month for some context about whether this difference is significant:


Code
library(ggplot2)

brfss_days <- brfss_svy |>
  srvyr::as_survey_design() |>
  srvyr::survey_count(age_group, sample_year, drink_days) |>
  tidyr::drop_na(drink_days) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    drink_days_cat = dplyr::case_when(
      drink_days == 0 ~ "0",
      drink_days <= 5 ~ "1-5",
      drink_days <= 10 ~ "6-10",
      drink_days <= 15 ~ "11-15",
      drink_days <= 20 ~ "16-20",
      drink_days <= 25 ~ "21-25",
      drink_days <= 30 ~ "26-30"
    ) |>
      forcats::fct(
        levels = c("0", "1-5", "6-10", "11-15", "16-20", "21-25", "26-30")
      )
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    p = n / sum(n),
    .by = c(age_group, sample_year)
  )

compare_distributions <- function(x) {
  ggplot() +
    geom_col(
      data = dplyr::filter(x, sample_year == "2011"),
      mapping = aes(x = drink_days_cat, y = p, fill = sample_year),
      alpha = 0.5
    ) +
    geom_col(
      data = dplyr::filter(x, sample_year == "2024"),
      mapping = aes(x = drink_days_cat, y = p, fill = sample_year),
      alpha = 0.3
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent()) +
    labs(
      x = "Drinking days per month",
      fill = "Sample year",
      y = NULL
    )
}

brfss_days |>
  dplyr::filter(age_group == "Age 21-27") |>
  compare_distributions() +
  labs(title = "Age 21-27")









Code
brfss_days |>
  dplyr::filter(age_group == "Age 28+") |>
  compare_distributions() +
  labs(title = "Age 28+")









We can see in the distributions that the change is actually more pronounced for Age 21-27 than for Age 28+, so that supports our hypothesis that the difference in differences is significant. Our second research question was: Does Gen Z drink less often? We can answer yes.


Binge drinking
Proportion of participants who binge drink:

  formula = binge_drink ~ age_group * sample_year,
  design = brfss_svy
)

The difference of differences here for binge drinking is 9.8% and our p-value is <0.001, so we can conclude that it’s statistically significant. Our third research question was: Is Gen Z less likely to binge drink? We can confidently answer yes.
To recap:
Compared to previous generations at the same age…

Is Gen Z more likely to be completely sober? (meaning, to not drink at all)? Yes.
Does Gen Z drink less often? Yes.
Is Gen Z less likely to binge drink? Yes.

There are plenty of practical applications of this knowledge, particularly for public health. In my everyday life, it means I’m happy to see more NA options on the drinks menu!
